speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you were saying you were-- now are-- you've left Duvno. And you're in Lvov. Do you want to just continue from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So now, me and my-- my sister and my brother and me, we left to Poland on a train. And we came to a town, Byten. And my brother got out and got an apartment, where to live, you know, rented an apartment. And, and I went out, you know, on the streets to see what's going on. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Lemberg, though, in Lvov? Do-- any--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lemberg, I was there from-- I was in Dubno only maybe four, five months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I think we went out from Lemberg-- beginning of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, beginning of '45. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or '44-- we left the end of '44, yeah. And, when we came to Byten, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I met a friend on the streets. I was walking in the street. There was nothing to do. I met a friend from Dubno who I recognized. And I said, what are you doing? He, he had a sister-- also left from the Holocaust. And he says, the Jewish agency organizes what they call 'kibbutz.' And they teach you. There's a school. They teach you-- teach you Zionism. And we're going to emigrate to Israel. That's our country. And we're going to build our country. Come on. Come over. I'll show it to you. And I went over, and I looked at it. And I loved it right away. It was mostly my age kids. They went through schooling. It was-- and then dances and songs, which I didn't have for a long time. I went back and told my sister, I'm going there to live with them. And she agreed. She says, fine. And every weekend, I'm going to visit you, you know. But I'll be there. I'll be sleeping there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came in. And right away, they looked for children. A matter of fact-- that her brother helped a lot of the kids who were hidden with Gentile families. And he knew, after the war, when I met him in Poland-- he used to do that, bring kids to those kibbutzim-- survivors. But anyways, I was there. And I started a new life, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- and my sister decided to go to Germany. And she left with her husband. And I stayed with the kibbutz for a while. And then one day, we said that we go in illegal. We have to get to Germany through Czechoslovakia, Austria. And it's going to be all illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So don't speak on the train. If a Russian soldier comes over and ask you, speak Hebrew-- yeah, whatever you know, prayers, so they wouldn't understand you. But don't speak Polish. Because they'll ask you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so we came to Czechoslovakia. We crossed the border. From there, to Austria-- we travelled a few days on the train. And in Austria, we stayed, like, a week. And we continued. And we came to Germany-- and to a town, Rosenheim. It was a teenage DP camp, mostly from kibbutzim from all kind of-- that I was in, in a movement which are called [NON-ENGLISH]. There were a movement Zehut, all kind of parties. And I was there in a group. And they gave us education, prepared us to emigrate to Israel. And then one-- and a year later-- excuse me-- I found out where my sister lives. She lived in a little town, Pocking. I found out, and I used to visit her from time to time. I was with her, I remember, two months, went back. And one day, let's all get ready to go. We're going to France. So we left Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this is your sister who's going to France.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh no, the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left. And I called-- and I visit her, I told her, we're going to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to France in the outskirt home. They kept us there, maybe 120 children. And every night, they told us that we're going to go on a boat to Israel. And one day, one night, they told us, get on the trucks. And we came to Marseilles. And they loaded us on a ship. And the name was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they loaded us. And about 2 o'clock in the morning, the, the ship left the port of Marseilles with 4,500 people. They came from all over. They gathered them from all over-- mostly Polish, Romanian, Czechoslovakian Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: All ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: All ages, the children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm

speaker: interviewee
text: --and women. And apparently, it was a boat that travelled on the Mississippi River. It was bought in the United States. And volunteers from the-- from-- Jewish people from the Army, Marines, they volunteered, fixed it up, brought it over to Italy, fixed it up, brought it to France. Among them was one Gentile priest. Oh god, I forgot his name. I just saw him in the United States before he died. He was-- in Allentown. And we had a nice talk. He was among them. And it was some small accident to come out from the port. And then we pulled out. And as we pulled out the next day, we saw a military English [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ship following us. The next day, two ships. The third day, three ships, till we were surrounded by eleven ships. And a day before we came to the border-- they used to come very near the boat and tell us, just give in, you'll never get to the-- to the Palestine borders. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That is not-- it doesn't pay in there. But we were very well organized by, by underground people from Israel, by the Haganah. And the night before we have to arrive, around 12 o'clock, 1 o'clock, broke out the first fight. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They came near in the-- they threw anchor. They tore apart the ship. And we threw back tomatoes, potatoes, cans, whatever we could. And what happened is one ship used to hit the The used to tilt. They used to jump on the boat. They used to throw them in the water. They were shooting, but not real bullets. Once we found out that they, they make a lot of noise, the rubber bullets, we fought harder. But apparently, one guy who didn't want to fall in the water, he, he shot and he killed-- two people were killed. One of my best friends, from my kibbutz, not far from me, was killed. And after two, three hours of fighting, the ship was torn apart, you know, the kids, children. And so they, they put around ropes and chains. And they dragged us into the port, surrounded by very heavy British army. The next morning, they took us off the ships. And they divided us in three, three ships, which were jails. And, and late afternoon, out to the sea. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The ship-- in each cell was 800 people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the deck, there were toilets. From the beginning, they tried-- we were asking, we thought we were going to Cyprus. Because usual, all the illegal ships used to go to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we saw that the Cyprus, overnight-- you know, we should be in Cyprus over a day, you know? It only takes so many hours. But we saw-- no, we are in the ocean. And the British soldiers used to have a gate. And they used to say, six people up, six people go back, six people up. Well how do you control a basement of 800 people with six people up? So after a few days, we saw that we're not going to Cyprus. And we didn't know where we're going. It broke out-- the first fight. We want to go up on the deck whenever we can. We don't want to let the soldiers watching us going up the deck, going down the deck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we refused to eat. They brought out soup. We threw it out through the deck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the hunger strike?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the hunger strike, yes. There was many hunger strikes. But the first hunger strike broke out right on the second day. We wouldn't eat. And we-- they gave it. They let us go out out the deck.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the organization on-- in terms of the discipline on the ship itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your youth leaders--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you see a lot of--

speaker: interviewer
text: --maintain control?

speaker: interviewee
text: --a lot of--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of the-- a lot of the kibbutzim-- each kibbutz had a leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a lot of kibbutzim, a lot from kibbutz-- from [NON-ENGLISH], from [NON-ENGLISH], you know? Young-- and there was also adults that were on their own. It didn't took long. Right away, they were-- we were organized. They choose their leaders. We were perfect organized. We listened to each other. We were disciplined. We knew what we're doing. We had one enemy to fight. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the first hunger strike, they let us go up on the deck. And we start have a little, little bit freedom. After-- but we saw the other two ships from a distance afar, oh god, 2,000 feet. But there was no communication. And we were all three going-- three jail ships.

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- I think after 10 or 12 days, we arrived in France in a little port. They call it Port-de-Bouc, not far from France. And then planes starting to come. Photographers, they wanted to know. And they start questioning. Well, the answer was that we were fishermen. The British took us out from the sea. We didn't come from France. But the fight between the British government and the French was, look, they left France. We want them back in France. The French government offered us citizenship in one year if we get off the boats. We refused. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we stayed six weeks in the port. Food was plentiful because the agencies and supporters sent plenty of food. And one day-- afternoon, they just took those anchors up. And they told us, we're taking you back to Germany. And there, we're going to unload you. See, Germany was divided in three zones-- four zones, I'm sorry. It was British, English. They took us to Hamburg. Now, when they took us, we start getting a little scared because, you know, we gave them a lot of problems. Now we didn't know how they were going to react. And when we arrived in, in Hamburg in the late afternoon, they told us, get ready. Tomorrow morning, you're going to leave the ships. And we're going to take you on trains. You're leaving the ships. We said, we're not leaving the ships. We're going back to Israel-- but it was Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got off the steps in early morning. They came with heavy water pressurized pipes-- the hoses.

speaker: interviewer
text: The hoses, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was terrible. It was terrible. When you get hit with it, I mean, you, you felt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just fall over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they lowered down ropes. A lot of British soldiers, drunk, half a tire, sticks-- and they beat us up. They separated us and took  us on top of the wrists to trains. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they took us in trains to a little-- I was in a little village, Poppendorf, surrounded with barbed wires, machine guns, right back--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right back, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [SOB] And we had a lot of support from Jewish agencies, from Joint. After being two weeks, we woke up one morning, and we  were on our own. They just left us. They couldn't get out nothing of us. So they just left us. And we started to--

speaker: interviewer
text: In this camp with the barbed wire--

speaker: interviewee
text: In that camp. We woke up in the morning-- there was nobody there.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and no guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody holding you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The judge decided that's enough of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Jewish agency where they held-- we shall spread them out again. And because I-- there were preferences, people that they send them to Israel because they need young people, you know, to-- probably to organize ourselves to the army. So I was going on a older man who died in Israel, and his certificate, you know, and his document, like. What happened, apparently, is many people who died in Israel, they didn't even register them. And then they used to take his papers and send it in. And they used to come. So I came with a French ship, back to France. Then in France I wound up on a ship. And I came to Israel. We were a whole group. And I went to one detention camp for a few days. And then with a boss to our kibbutz in Israel in Upper Galilee, by the Golan Heights. They call it [NON-ENGLISH]. They had already some kids in that group. So we were 40 kids-- 20 girls and 20 boys. They called it the Polish-- the Polish-Russian group. And we were supposed to learn four hours and, and work four hours. But in May-- I arrived in February 1948. Back in '47, the came to Israel. So-- and then by the time I went back and I came back, it was already '48, in February. In May, the British left already. Palestine--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and Israel had their own countries, own government. And the war broke out, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were settled in Israel by that time--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already in Israel, in the youth group. And as the, the war broke out, we were near the Golan Heights. That was the main spot right away. So no more school-- it was strictly fighting in the front line. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Syrian army was 800 feet from our kibbutz. Matter of fact, where I was in the bunker, there was a gal who lives, too, in Israel. She threw a Molotov grenade and burned the, the-- the tank came already to the kibbutz. A lot of members had children, couldn't-- they, they were scared, they want to leave it. He came out. And he supported them. He says, you're not going to leave. You're going to protect it. We fought back by burning a few tanks. They run back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, and we were taken each time-- let's say, where there was the, the war of the Upper Galilee to, to, to conquer, to take back the Upper Galilee. I was taken to the army. They used to come to each kibbutz and take certain of them. I was in the Upper Galilee. And then they released me back to the kibbutz. And after being two years-- and the war was finished. There was a ceasefire. They told us that we have to go and help a young kibbutz. This was a older kibbutz already that was there for many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were all of 18, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we, we were all 18, 20 years by now-- 18, 19, 20. We went-- this was in-- end of '49. We went to build-- to our kibbutz. It was very few people near the Galilee Sea. The name was Hakuk. And it was mostly-- it was mostly Israeli-born people. It was a little difficult because of the mentality. Holocaust survivors, the zabris, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a while, I met some people. Among them was her brother, who I knew him from Germany yet. And they told me that they're building a new kibbutz, which going to be from the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising. And most of the people, that's from the Holocaust survivors. And I decided to leave this kibbutz and go there. And I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you talked about the, the tension between, between the survivors and somewhat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I told you, it was a little bit in the beginning like we segregated ourselves in separate rooms. They segregated themselves in separate rooms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had mixed feelings about the Holocaust. The question was, how could you go like animals-- how?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which-- I could understand it. But they never went through. They couldn't understand. How does a mother with four kids fight whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So well a little, a little tension. But-- so I went to the kibbutz from the Holocaust survivors. And we built up kibbutz. And her brother was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was with me in the army later. And, and then one day, he came, and he told me that he found his sisters through the Red Cross. And through the government and a lot of help, he went. And sure enough, he brought them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came. And I happened to meet her. And I spoke Polish. And was a very nice girl. Right away, we, we happened to like each other. And then it got to be when, when she becomes 18, she has to go to the army. And I didn't want her to go to the army. So when she got the pink slip to go to the army, we decided to get married. So we got married in the kibbutz. And we lived in the kibbutz for a year and a half. And then she didn't like it too much. It was-- see, she came from a different background, which you probably heard her. You come to a pioneering place, which you really have to sacrifice mostly your life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's, it's giving, and giving, and giving. And, and, and it's a socialistic way of-- the only way to achieve it is in a socialistic way. Because you, you have to give every-- one is for the other, you know? She grew up in a different atmosphere. She didn't just-- it wasn't there, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But she couldn't-- she told me, she cannot take it. So I-- through a friend, I found a job in the-- in, in the Israeli Soccer Association. And I got a job. And we had a little house, a shanty-- no water, no sewer, no hot water. But they gave us this little house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was the-- I managed 11 teams-- all the games, organized the games, the uniforms, the soccer field, the serving food. And I worked there from 1955 till '60. Very liked-- they liked me. And I liked them. I was-- I loved it. It's-- but it was nerve-racking. And, and then it started. We had a child-- nowhere to live, no conditions. So it was either me coming to United States or my sister coming there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you know, my sister wouldn't leave and come there. So her husband died. And she remarried. So one day, we decided that we're going to go to United States. And we started to arrange the papers. And a year later, we came to United States. And you got it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And whereabouts in the States did you come into?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came here. We started in Elizabeth, New Jersey. I was in Elizabeth, New Jersey from 1960. In 1960, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I worked certain places. And then 1963, I was given a chance by some people to go into the building business. I took a chance. I did, I succeeded, and then I went. And I moved in 1965 to Pennsylvania, to Allentown. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I moved because of the business. And we stayed-- we stayed there. We stayed there till now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of business? Oh, the building and construction?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the building-- developing and building business, yeah. So in United States, in Elizabeth, was born the second son. When we came with our first son from Israel, he was two and a half. Was born, the other son. They're both married. I have three grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spend part of the year in Florida?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wintertime, yeah. Back and forth, I'm back and forth. I'm going back-- back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you got the whole story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Got the whole story. Is there anything-- just in terms of closing, if there's a message to people other than your family who would see this tape, what would you want them to know, bottom line about the Holocaust, the most important thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mainly did it because-- I didn't even know that I'm going to be doing that. I did it because my son called out, and he insisted. He also insisted a few years ago to go back to Poland, the whole family-- my both sons, my wife. And he wanted to see and make a tape where she lived in Warsaw. And he's supposed to go from that to Brody. And the visas came too late, so we had to turn back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So have you ever been back to Brody?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going back in July with my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: In July, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's buried there. We're probably going to find-- try to find the grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I mainly did it for my sons, for my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, I thank you for doing it. It's important that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --we know this. Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I do now?

speaker: interviewer
text: When he says ready, you just-- you introduce Edna and tell when you were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Edna Brill.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her in 1952.

speaker: interviewee
text: One. One.

speaker: interviewee
text: One?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we married in 1953 in Israel in a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kibbutz [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And we married 42 and 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 42-- [LAUGHS] not and 'a half.'

speaker: interviewee
text: What else? We have three-- two, two sons and three grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two, two daughter-in-laws.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two daughter-in-laws.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're very important here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now you're on camera. Do you love me? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that many years-- tell them if you're happily married, come on, then. Do I have to push you?

words.text: Do I have to push you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 42 and 1/2 years. Do I have to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 42, not 'and a half'-- January 30th, so January, February, March.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Edna and Harry Brill the day of the wedding, 1953. In Nahariya, Israel. This is our 35th wedding anniversary in Miami Beach, Florida. To your left is Sammy and Rachel, who was two months old, and Janet, his wife; me and my wife; and Eli, our younger son. This is a picture of Sam Brill to your left, who is our older son; in the middle, Eli Brill, who was our younger son; and Janet [NON-ENGLISH]. This picture was taken in Philadelphia. It is September the 6th at the wedding of our younger son, Eli. To your left is Harry Brill; his wife, [? Eileen; ?] Eli Brill; and his mother, Edna Brill. This is our granddaughter, [? Rachel, ?] who's seven years old. This is [? Jared ?] [? Benjamin ?] Brill. And he's three months old-- our grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is [? Mia ?] Brill, five years old, also a grandchild. Thank you.
